source.title: Diagnosis and Management of Acute Diverticulitis

signals: Diagnosis and Management of Acute Diverticulitis
Hospitalization should be considered if patients have signs of peritonitis
Inpatient management includes intravenous fluid resuscitation and intravenous antibiotics
Fifteen to 30 percent of patients admitted with acute diverticulitis require surgical intervention

extraction.summary: Acute diverticulitis is inflammation of the colonic diverticulum that may involve perforation or microperforation. It can be uncomplicated (localized inflammation) or complicated (associated with abscess, phlegmon, fistula, obstruction, bleeding, or perforation). Most patients can be managed outpatient with supportive care, while complications may require hospitalization, antibiotics, drainage procedures, or surgery.

population.age_criteria: Predominantly affects patients >45 years (5-10% prevalence), increasing to 80% in those >85 years
population.risk_criteria: Risk factors include increasing age, obesity, sedentary lifestyle, NSAIDs, aspirin use, smoking, genetic susceptibility
population.disease_definition: Acute inflammation of colonic diverticulum with or without complications (abscess, perforation, fistula, obstruction, bleeding)
population.exclusions: Children and pregnant women excluded from this protocol
population.severity_staging: Uncomplicated (localized inflammation) vs Complicated (with abscess, phlegmon, fistula, obstruction, bleeding, or perforation)

key_thresholds.context: Typical fever usually below this threshold
Diagnostic threshold with LLQ tenderness and absence of vomiting
Present in 55% of acute diverticulitis patients
Lower recurrence risk if ≥50 years (12.2% vs 16.2%)

recommendations.recommendation: Antibiotics not recommended for mild uncomplicated diverticulitis
recommendations.notes: Recent trials show no acceleration of recovery, prevention of complications, or recurrences

recommendations.recommendation: CT is the test of choice for suspected diverticulitis
recommendations.notes: Excellent diagnostic accuracy in 8 studies with 684 patients

recommendations.recommendation: Colonoscopy 4-6 weeks after resolution for complicated disease
Laparoscopic surgery preferred over open colectomy
recommendations.notes: Shorter stay, fewer complications, lower mortality

recommendations.recommendation: Abdominal CT is the test of choice for suspected diverticulitis
Outpatient management with rest and fluids for mild diverticulitis
Inpatient management for moderate to severe diverticulitis

recommendations.notes: Must cover gram-negative rods and anaerobes

recommendations.recommendation: Antibiotics may not be necessary for uncomplicated outpatient diverticulitis
Laparoscopic surgery preferred over open colectomy when surgery needed
recommendations.notes: Shorter stay, fewer complications, lower mortality

medications.dose: Cipro 750 mg q12h + Flagyl 500 mg q6h orally
3.375 g IV q6h or 4.5 g IV q8h
Short course IV followed by oral

red_flags: Signs of peritonitis (rebound tenderness, rigidity, absent bowel sounds)
Free air on imaging suggesting perforation
Severe abdominal pain with rebound tenderness
extraction.follow_up: Outpatient follow-up in 2-3 days for mild cases. Clinical improvement expected within 2-4 days for hospitalized patients with decreasing fever, leukocytosis, and pain. Colonoscopy 4-6 weeks after resolution for complicated disease.
extraction.escalation_path: Hospitalize if signs of peritonitis or suspected complicated diverticulitis. Surgical consultation for: persistent symptoms despite conservative management, large abscess requiring drainage, perforation, bowel obstruction, or fistula formation. 15-30% of admitted patients require surgical intervention during admission.
